title: Inside the Unsolved Gardner Museum Heist
summary: In 1990, two men disguised as police officers stole 13 artworks from the Isabella Stewart Gardner Museum in Boston. The heist remains the largest art theft in history, with the artworks valued at over $500 million. The thieves entered by tricking a security guard, then handcuffed the guards and cut paintings from their frames. Stolen works included Vermeer's 'The Concert' and Rembrandt's 'Christ in the Storm on the Sea of Galilee', along with pieces by Degas and Manet. A Napoleonic eagle finial was also taken. The museum has offered a $10 million reward. The FBI has identified suspects but not publicly named them. Former FBI agent Geoff Kelly wrote a book detailing the investigation and naming the men he believes responsible. The case remains open, and the empty frames still hang in the museum as a reminder.

background_read: The Isabella Stewart Gardner Museum was designed to resemble a Venetian palace. Gardner stipulated in her will that nothing be changed after her death. The theft occurred on March 18, 1990, after St. Patrick's Day celebrations. The FBI investigation has lasted decades, and the case remains one of the most famous unsolved art crimes.
Article_Structure: The article covers the 1990 Gardner Museum heist, the largest art theft. Main points: thieves disguised as police, stole 13 works including Vermeer and Rembrandt, worth over $500M. Purpose: to inform about the heist and a new book revealing suspect names. Evidence: based on FBI investigation and former agent's account. Author credibility: Geoff Kelly is a former FBI agent who led the investigation. Methodology: Kelly reviewed case files and interviewed sources.

perspectives.description: Believes they know who did it but cannot publicly name them.
Offers reward and keeps empty frames as tribute.
